title: Addition
shortDescription: A parent's guide to teaching addition with hands-on objects, how carrying works in two-digit problems, and the slips that trip up beginners.
importance: Addition is the first operation children meet, and it is the foundation for everything that follows, since subtraction, multiplication, and division all lean on it. A child who is confident with addition steps into each new topic on the right foot, while a child who hesitates carries that struggle forward. It also turns up constantly in daily life, working out the total for several items, scoring a game, or counting how many people are coming for dinner. It is worth building real understanding here, not just memorizing answers.
howToTeach: Start with physical objects like blocks, pencils, or candies so your child can watch the addition actually happen.
Explain that addition means putting things together, and that the total is always larger than each of the numbers.
Practice small numbers up to 10 first, and only move on once that feels easy and automatic.
When your child is ready, introduce carrying gradually, where the units pass 10 and you move a ten over to the next column.
Use a number line as a visual aid, jumping forward from one spot and counting the steps.

examples.explanation: Start at 7 and count on five more: 8, 9, 10, 11, 12. Or use the make-ten trick: 7 + 3 = 10, then add the remaining 2 to get 12.
Units first: 5 + 8 = 13, so write 3 and carry 1. Then tens: 2 + 3 plus the carried 1 makes 6. The answer is 63.
A handy trick: complete the 9 up to 10 using 1 from the 6, then 10 plus the remaining 5 gives 15.
Adding whole tens is like adding 4 + 6 and then attaching a zero. Since 4 + 6 = 10, the result is 100.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to carry when the units pass 10, so 25 + 38 comes out as 53 instead of 63.
Mixing up the order of digits when writing vertically, instead of lining units under units.
Skipping a number while counting forward, which gives an answer that is one too high or one too low.
Counting the starting number itself, so 7 + 5 becomes 11 instead of 12.
parentTips: Do not rush, and give your child a few seconds to think even when the wait feels long.
Encourage using fingers at the start, because it is a healthy and legitimate counting tool.
Turn practice into a game: I have 3 apples and you give me 4 more, how many do we have now?
Celebrate small wins, since the feeling of progress is the fuel that keeps a child going.
When there is a mistake, do not correct it right away, just ask are you sure, want to count again?
